skill.name: scanpy
skill.description: Standard single-cell RNA-seq analysis pipeline. For quality control (QC), normalization, dimensionality reduction (PCA/UMAP/t-SNE), clustering, differential expression analysis, and visualization. Best suited for exploratory single-cell transcriptomics analysis using established workflows. For deep learning models, use scvi-tools; for data format issues, use anndata.
skill.summary: End-to-end single-cell RNA-seq analysis using scanpy: QC, normalization, clustering, visualization, and cell type annotation.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 244 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The 11 HIGH severity 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags are false matches on bioinformatics terminology ('cell barcodes') and docstring comments. All 226 MEDIUM 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' flags are markdown code fence blocks (```python) misinterpreted as shell execution. The 2 filesystem flags are legitimate os.makedirs() calls for saving analysis outputs. The 5 network flags are documentation reference URLs to scanpy official sites. The 3 'system reconnaissance' flags come from a JSON audit results file. No actual security threats were confirmed. The skill is a legitimate bioinformatics pipeline for single-cell RNA-seq analysis.

content.user_title: Analyze Single-Cell RNA-seq Data with Scanpy
content.value_statement: Single-cell RNA-seq analysis requires complex multi-step workflows that are hard to execute reliably without deep bioinformatics expertise. This skill provides a complete scanpy pipeline with quality control, normalization, clustering, visualization, and cell type annotation built into a single workflow.
actual_capabilities: Perform quality control on single-cell RNA-seq data with automated filtering of low-quality cells and genes
Execute normalization, scaling, and dimensionality reduction including PCA, UMAP, and t-SNE
Identify cell clusters using Leiden algorithm and find differentially expressed marker genes
Annotate cell types based on gene expression marker patterns
Generate publication-quality visualizations including UMAP plots, violin plots, heatmaps, and dot plots
Run trajectory inference with PAGA and diffusion pseudotime for developmental studies
limitations: Does not include deep learning models for data analysis; use scvi-tools for that purpose
Cannot handle data format conversion between incompatible formats; use anndata for that purpose
Does not support spatial transcriptomics data analysis
Requires pre-processed count matrices or h5ad files as input data

use_cases.title: Quality Control for Single-Cell RNA-seq Data
use_cases.description: A researcher needs to filter low-quality cells and genes from a newly sequenced scRNA-seq dataset before performing downstream analysis.
use_cases.target_user: Bench scientist with sequencing data needing data cleaning

use_cases.title: Cell Type Identification from Gene Expression Data
use_cases.description: A bioinformatician wants to identify and annotate distinct cell populations from tumor microenvironment gene expression data.
use_cases.target_user: Computational biologist analyzing cancer tissue samples

use_cases.title: Publication-Ready Single-Cell Visualizations
use_cases.description: A graduate student needs to create professional UMAP plots, heatmaps, and dot plots for a research paper submission.
use_cases.target_user: Academic researcher preparing figures for publication

prompt_templates.title: Basic Quality Control on scRNA-seq Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Run quality control on the single-cell RNA-seq data from the file at [input_path]. Use the qc_analysis.py script with mitochondrial threshold of [mt_threshold], minimum [min_genes] genes per cell, and minimum [min_cells] cells per gene. Generate QC plots showing the distributions before and after filtering.
prompt_templates.scenario: Filtering raw single-cell data for the first time

prompt_templates.title: Complete Analysis Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a complete single-cell RNA-seq analysis on the data from [input_file]. Follow the standard workflow in assets/analysis_template.py: load data, run quality control, normalize, identify highly variable genes, scale data, compute PCA and UMAP, cluster cells with Leiden algorithm at resolution [resolution], find marker genes, and save results to [output_dir].
prompt_templates.scenario: Running a full end-to-end single-cell analysis

prompt_templates.title: Custom Cell Type Annotation
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the processed data at [input_file]. Cluster the cells and find marker genes for each cluster. Use the following known cell type markers to annotate the clusters: [list known markers]. Create a UMAP plot colored by cell type and save the annotated data.
prompt_templates.scenario: Annotating cell types using known marker gene references

prompt_templates.title: Trajectory Analysis with Differential Expression
prompt_templates.prompt: Load the AnnData file at [input_file]. Run trajectory inference using PAGA with the existing Leiden clusters. Compute diffusion pseudotime using cluster [root_cluster] as the root. Generate plots of pseudotime on UMAP. Then perform differential expression analysis comparing cells in clusters [cluster_a] and [cluster_b] using the Wilcoxon method.
prompt_templates.scenario: Investigating developmental trajectories and gene expression differences

output_examples.input: Run QC on my single-cell data from file counts.h5ad with default thresholds.
output: The quality control report shows 12,500 cells with 34,000 genes. After filtering, 11,230 cells (89.8%) and 28,000 genes (82.4%) remain. Key metrics: mean genes per cell is 1,850, mean mitochondrial percentage is 3.2%. Violin plots and scatter plots are saved in the figures/ directory.

output_examples.input: Find cell types in the processed data and generate a UMAP visualization.
output: UMAP visualization reveals 8 distinct clusters. Marker gene analysis identifies Cluster 0 as CD4+ T cells (markers: CD3D, CD3E), Cluster 1 as CD14+ Monocytes (markers: CD14, LYZ), and Cluster 2 as B cells (markers: MS4A1, CD79A). Cell type annotations have been applied. The annotated data is saved to results/processed_data.h5ad.

best_practices: Always save raw counts with adata.raw = adata before filtering to enable expression visualization and analysis on non-normalized data later.
Check the PCA variance contribution elbow plot to determine the optimal number of principal components for neighborhood computation.
Try multiple clustering resolutions from 0.3 to 1.2 to find the biologically meaningful granularity for your specific dataset.
anti_patterns: Do not skip quality control steps or apply default thresholds without reviewing the data distribution plots first.
Do not run differential expression analysis without ensuring data is properly normalized and batch effects are addressed.
Do not annotate cell types based on a single marker gene; always validate with multiple established markers.

faq.question: What input formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill supports h5ad (AnnData), 10X Genomics HDF5 (.h5), 10X mtx directory, and CSV file formats.

faq.question: How do I choose the mitochondrial threshold for quality control?
faq.answer: Start with a threshold of 5% for most human tissues. Adjust up to 20% for tissues with high metabolic activity such as heart or kidney.

faq.question: What is the recommended clustering method for this skill?
faq.answer: Leiden clustering is recommended over Louvain for better modularity optimization and more reproducible results.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for non-human single-cell data?
faq.answer: Yes, but adjust the mitochondrial gene prefix from MT- to the appropriate species prefix such as mt- for mouse.

faq.question: How do I save intermediate results during the workflow?
faq.answer: Use adata.write() to save the full AnnData object at any step. Export individual tables with adata.obs.to_csv() and adata.var.to_csv().

faq.question: What is the difference between this skill and scvi-tools skills?
faq.answer: This skill uses scanpy for standard statistical analysis including QC and clustering. For deep learning models, use scvi-tools instead.
